resources.string: Follow the on-screen guidance or performed standard movement patterns.
Follow proper form and control your breathing.
Focus on controlled motion and avoid using momentum.
Start your first workout to see your progress chart!
Prepare your body for the main workout.
Great job! You have completed today\'s session.
Recovery, walking, light mobility. Give your muscles time to grow.
Enable and complete extra posture sessions to see separate progress here.
Keep your streak alive. Start your daily workout now.
Correct your posture with these daily exercises.
Browse a fixed curated list of exercises by training type and body area.
No exercises match the selected filters.
Adaptive 3-day meal planning that follows your workout schedule and program progress.
See only today’s meals with simple preparation steps and mark them off as you eat.
No meal plan is available for today yet.
Combined ingredients for all 3 days, grouped by what to buy.
Grill the chicken until fully cooked.
Put the yogurt or cottage cheese into a bowl.
Add the remaining foods and serve the meal.
Serve the meal and eat while warm.
Oatmeal with milk, banana, peanut butter, and whey protein.
Eggs, whole-grain toast, and Greek yogurt.
Cottage cheese with berries, honey, and mixed nuts.
Chicken breast, rice, olive oil, and avocado salad.
Lean beef pasta with tomato sauce and parmesan.
Turkey meatballs with quinoa and vegetables.
Tofu or tempeh bowl with rice and edamame.
Tuna wraps with cheese and yogurt sauce.
Whey shake with banana and oats.
Greek yogurt with granola and fruit.
Chocolate milk and a cottage cheese sandwich.
Embark on a structured 8-week program designed to transform your strength and mobility. Follow daily workouts, track your progress, and stay consistent with our streak system.
A fundamental upper body exercise that strengthens the chest, shoulders, and triceps while engaging the core.

1. Start in a high plank position with hands slightly wider than shoulders.
2. Lower your body until your chest nearly touches the floor.
3. Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels.
4. Push yourself back up to the starting position.
Keep elbows at a 45-degree angle to your torso. Engage your glutes and core to maintain a rigid body line. Focus on a full range of motion.
Sagging hips, flared elbows (90 degrees), looking up instead of keeping neck neutral, and restricted range of motion.
A powerful compound exercise that targets the quadriceps, hamstrings, and glutes.

1. Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, toes slightly pointed out.
2. Inhale and lower your hips by bending knees as if sitting in a chair.
3. Go down until thighs are parallel to the floor or lower.
4. Exhale and drive through your heels to return to a standing position.
Keep your chest up and back flat. Your knees should track in line with your toes. Distribute weight evenly on your heels and mid-foot.
Knees caving inward, heels lifting off the floor, rounding the lower back, and not going deep enough.
The ultimate bodyweight back exercise, focusing on the latissimus dorsi, biceps, and grip strength.

1. Grab the bar with an overhand grip, slightly wider than shoulder-width.
2. Start from a dead hang with arms fully extended.
3. Pull your body up by driving elbows down until your chin is over the bar.
4. Lower yourself back down with control to the starting position.
Engage your shoulder blades by pulling them down and back before you start the pull. Avoid using momentum or swinging your legs (kipping).
Using momentum/swinging, not reaching full extension at the bottom, and shrugging shoulders toward ears.
A core stability exercise that builds isometric strength throughout the entire trunk.

1. Place forearms on the floor, elbows directly under shoulders.
2. Extend legs behind you, resting weight on toes.
3. Form a straight line from head to heels.
4. Hold the position while breathing deeply and maintaining tension.
Squeeze your glutes and quadriceps to stabilize the pelvis. Push away from the floor through your forearms to engage the serratus anterior.
Sagging hips, lifting the glutes too high (peaking), holding breath, and looking forward rather than down.
An effective exercise for isolating the triceps and building lower chest strength.

1. Grip parallel bars and lift yourself up with locked arms.
2. Lower your body by bending elbows until they are at a 90-degree angle.
3. Keep your torso slightly leaned forward for chest emphasis or upright for triceps.
4. Push back up to the starting position.
Keep your shoulders down and away from your ears. Ensure elbows don\'t flare out too much. Control the descent to protect the shoulder joint.
Flaring elbows, excessive swinging, and dipping too deep (below 90 degrees) which can strain shoulders.
A functional lower body movement that improves balance, coordination, and leg strength.

1. Stand tall and take a large step forward with one leg.
2. Lower your hips until both knees are bent at approximately 90 degrees.
3. Your back knee should hover just above the floor.
4. Push off the front foot to return to the starting position.
Keep your torso upright and core engaged. Ensure the front knee stays aligned with the foot and doesn\'t collapse inward.
Leaning too far forward, front knee passing the toes, and losing balance due to a narrow stance.
A pull exercise that builds thickness in the upper and middle back.

1. Hinge at the hips with a flat back, holding a weight or resistance band.
2. Pull the weight toward your lower ribs, squeezing shoulder blades together.
3. Hold for a second at the top of the movement.
4. Slowly lower the weight back to the starting position.
Keep your spine neutral throughout. Focus on pulling with your elbows rather than your hands. Do not use your lower back to swing the weight.
Rounding the back, using momentum, and not reaching a full range of motion.
An abdominal exercise that specifically targets the lower portion of the rectus abdominis.

1. Lie flat on your back with legs straight and hands by your sides or under your glutes.
2. Lift your legs toward the ceiling until they are at a 90-degree angle.
3. Slowly lower your legs back down without letting them touch the floor.
4. Keep your lower back pressed firmly into the ground.
Exhale as you lift and inhale as you lower. If your back arches, don\'t lower your legs as far. Focus on the tension in your lower abs.
Arching the lower back, lifting legs with momentum, and bending the knees.
A gentle flow that improves spinal mobility and coordinates movement with breath.

1. Start on all fours with wrists under shoulders and knees under hips.
2. Inhale: Drop your belly, lift your chest and tailbone (Cow).
3. Exhale: Round your back, tuck your chin and tailbone (Cat).
4. Repeat the flow smoothly with your breath.
Move slowly and feel each vertebra. Keep your arms straight throughout the entire movement. Focus on the transition between the two poses.
Moving too fast, bending the elbows, and forcing the range of motion.
A core exercise that emphasizes stability, balance, and coordination between the upper and lower body.

1. Start on all fours in a neutral spine position.
2. Simultaneously extend your right arm forward and left leg backward.
3. Hold for 2 seconds while maintaining a flat back.
4. Return to the starting position and switch sides.
Keep your hips and shoulders square to the floor. Imagine a glass of water on your back that you shouldn\'t spill. Do not lift your leg higher than hip level.
Arching the back, lifting the leg too high, and rotating the hips.
A comprehensive mobility drill that opens up the hips, thoracic spine, and hamstrings.

1. Step forward into a deep lunge.
2. Place the inside elbow to the instep of the front foot.
3. Rotate the same-side arm toward the ceiling, following with your eyes.
4. Return and step back, then switch sides.
Take your time in each position. Breathe deeply. Keep the back leg straight and active to maximize the hip flexor stretch.
Rushing through the movements and not reaching for full rotation.
A high-intensity, full-body exercise for conditioning and explosive power.

1. From a standing position, drop into a squat and place hands on the floor.
2. Kick your feet back into a plank and perform a push-up.
3. Jump your feet back toward your hands.
4. Explode upward into a jump with hands overhead.
Maintain a steady pace. Keep your core tight during the plank and push-up phases. Land softly on your feet after the jump.
Sagging hips during the push-up, landing flat-footed, and skipping the full range of motion.
A dynamic plank variation that challenges core stability while providing a cardiovascular stimulus.

1. Start in a high plank position.
2. Drive one knee toward your chest, then quickly switch legs.
3. Continue the "running" motion while keeping your hips stable.
4. Maintain a flat back throughout the exercise.
Keep your shoulders directly over your wrists. Minimize hip bounce. Focus on the "crunch" as the knee comes toward the chest.
Bouncing the hips, weight shifting too far back, and not bringing knees far enough forward.
An explosive hip-hinge movement that builds power in the posterior chain.

1. Stand with feet wider than shoulders, kettlebell on the floor in front of you.
2. Hinge at hips to grab the bell, then hike it between your legs.
3. Explosively snap your hips forward to swing the bell to chest height.
4. Let the bell fall back between your legs and repeat.
Power comes from the hips, not the arms. Arms should act only as "ropes." Keep your back flat and your core braced at the top.
Using arms to lift the weight, squatting instead of hinging, and rounding the back.
A focused exercise for isolating the glutes and strengthening the posterior chain.

1. Lie on your back with knees bent and feet flat on the floor, hip-width apart.
2. Drive through your heels to lift your hips toward the ceiling.
3. Squeeze your glutes hard at the top of the movement.
4. Slowly lower your hips back to the starting position.
Keep your core engaged to avoid overarching your lower back. Your shins should be vertical at the top of the movement. Do not let your knees cave in.
Overarching the lower back, lifting the heels off the floor, and not fully extending the hips.
Keep shoulders engaged, don\'t just dead hang.
Focus on rear delts and squeezing shoulder blades.

1. Anchor a band at face height and grab it with both hands.
2. Step back until the band is under tension with arms extended.
3. Pull the band toward your nose while driving elbows wide.
4. Pause briefly, then return with control.
Shrugging the shoulders, letting elbows drop, and using momentum instead of controlled upper-back movement.
A hanging drill that teaches shoulder blade control and improves upper-body posture.

1. Hang from a bar with straight arms and a firm grip.
2. Without bending the elbows, pull the shoulders down and slightly back.
3. Lift the body a few centimeters by moving only the shoulder blades.
4. Relax back into the hang and repeat smoothly.
Keep the ribs down and neck long. The movement should stay small and controlled, driven by the scapulae rather than the arms.
Bending the elbows, swinging the body, and shrugging toward the ears at the top.
A prone posture drill that opens the chest and strengthens the upper back.

1. Lie face down with arms by your sides and palms facing the floor.
2. Lift the hands slightly and sweep the arms overhead in a wide arc.
3. Keep the chest lightly lifted and squeeze the shoulder blades together.
4. Return the arms to the starting position with control.
Move slowly and keep the shoulders away from the ears. Reach long through the fingertips instead of jerking the arms upward.
Overarching the lower back, rushing the movement, and losing tension through the upper back.
A simple band exercise that reinforces scapular retraction and shoulder alignment.

1. Hold a light resistance band at shoulder height with straight arms.
2. Pull the band apart by moving the hands out to the sides.
3. Finish when the band reaches the chest and the shoulder blades squeeze together.
4. Return to the start under control and repeat.
Keep the ribs stacked over the pelvis and avoid arching the back. Think about moving from the upper back, not the wrists.
Bent wrists, shrugged shoulders, and letting the lower back overextend to finish the rep.
A movement designed to strengthen the lower back and improve posture by engaging the entire posterior chain.

1. Lie face down on the floor with arms extended overhead.
2. Simultaneously lift your arms, chest, and legs off the ground.
3. Hold the "flying" position for 2-3 seconds.
4. Slowly lower back down to the floor.
Keep your neck neutral by looking at the floor. Focus on the squeeze in your lower back and glutes. Move with control rather than using momentum.
Looking up too high, jerky movements, and holding breath.
An excellent drill for improving shoulder mobility and upper back posture.

1. Stand with your back against a wall, feet slightly forward.
2. Place your arms against the wall in a "W" shape (elbows and wrists touching the wall).
3. Slowly slide your arms up into a "Y" shape without losing contact with the wall.
4. Slide back down to the starting position.
Keep your lower back pressed into the wall. If your wrists or elbows lift off, only go as high as you can maintain contact. Quality over quantity.
Arching the lower back, allowing elbows/wrists to lift off the wall, and shrugging shoulders.
A controlled rotation drill for improving mobility through the upper spine.

1. Start on all fours and place one hand lightly behind your head.
2. Rotate the elbow down toward the supporting arm.
3. Open the chest by rotating the elbow up toward the ceiling.
4. Repeat on one side, then switch sides.
Keep the hips stable and let the movement come from the upper back. Follow the elbow with your eyes to encourage a full rotation.
Twisting through the lower back, moving too fast, and collapsing the supporting shoulder.
A front-body stretch that opens the abdominals and encourages spinal extension.

1. Lie face down with hands under the shoulders.
2. Press the floor away and lift the chest comfortably.
3. Keep the hips heavy and shoulders down.
4. Hold the position while breathing slowly, then lower back down.
Lift only as high as you can without pinching the lower back. Lengthen through the crown of the head and keep the elbows soft if needed.
Locking the elbows aggressively, shrugging the shoulders, and forcing a range that causes lower-back discomfort.
A restorative stretch that gently lengthens the back and helps relax the hips and shoulders.

1. Kneel on the floor and sit the hips back toward the heels.
2. Fold the torso forward and reach the arms long in front.
3. Let the forehead rest down and breathe into the ribcage.
4. Hold the stretch, then slowly rise back up.
Relax the shoulders and keep the breath slow. Widen the knees if you need more space for the torso.
Holding tension in the neck, bouncing into the stretch, and forcing the hips to the heels when mobility is limited.
An upper-back mobility drill that restores extension through the thoracic spine.

1. Sit or kneel tall with hands supporting the back of the head.
2. Lift the chest and gently extend over the upper back.
3. Pause briefly without flaring the ribs excessively.
4. Return to neutral and repeat with control.
Keep the lower back quiet and focus on opening through the sternum. Think about length before extension.
Turning it into a lower-back bend, pulling the head forward, and rushing the movement.
A half-kneeling stretch that targets the front of the hip and improves hip extension.

1. Step into a half-kneeling lunge with one knee on the floor.
2. Tuck the pelvis slightly and squeeze the glute of the rear leg.
3. Shift the hips forward until you feel a stretch at the front of the hip.
4. Hold, then switch sides.
Keep the torso tall and the ribs stacked over the pelvis. A gentle glute squeeze on the trailing leg increases the stretch without leaning forward.
Overarching the lower back, lunging too far forward, and collapsing through the front knee.
A seated hip mobility drill that trains both internal and external rotation.

1. Sit on the floor with both knees bent to 90 degrees, one leg in front and one to the side.
2. Sit tall and lean slightly over the front shin.
3. Rotate through the hips to switch both knees to the other side.
4. Repeat slowly, staying controlled throughout.
Keep the sit bones grounded as much as possible and move from the hips rather than throwing the knees around.
Rounding the back heavily, forcing the knees down, and rushing the side-to-side transition.
An advanced push-up variation that shifts the focus toward the shoulders and upper chest.

1. Start in a downward dog position with hips high in the air.
2. Lower the top of your head toward the floor between your hands.
3. Push back up until your arms are fully extended.
4. Keep your legs as straight as possible throughout.
Form a tripod with your head and hands at the bottom of the movement. Tuck your elbows slightly inward. The more vertical your torso, the harder the exercise.
Flaring elbows, letting hips sag, and lowering the head behind the hands rather than forward.
A traditional isometric hold that builds incredible leg strength and hip mobility.

1. Stand with feet double shoulder-width apart, toes pointed slightly outward.
2. Sink your hips down until thighs are parallel to the floor.
3. Keep your back straight and hands in front or on your hips.
4. Hold the position for the required time while breathing deeply.
Tuck your pelvis slightly to avoid overarching the lower back. Distribute weight evenly across the entire foot. Focus on a steady, calm breath.
Letting the knees collapse inward, rounding the back, and not sinking deep enough.
Sit deep in a squat position.
Keep heels on the ground and chest up.

1. Stand with feet slightly wider than hips and toes turned out as needed.
2. Lower into your deepest comfortable squat.
3. Keep the heels grounded and chest lifted.
4. Hold the position while breathing steadily.
Letting the heels pop up, collapsing the chest forward, and forcing depth without control.
A classic stretch for the back of the thigh that can help improve hip hinge comfort.

1. Extend one leg in front with the heel on the floor or a low surface.
2. Keep the back flat and hinge forward from the hips.
3. Stop when you feel a stretch along the back of the thigh.
4. Hold, then switch sides.
Lead with the chest instead of rounding the back. A small bend in the knee is fine if it helps keep the spine long.
Rounding the spine, locking the knee aggressively, and bouncing into the stretch.
